intro: The gradient of a graph tells you how fast one quantity is changing compared to another. In real-life graphs, the gradient has a specific meaning depending on what the axes represent.
explanation:
Gradient = change in y divided by change in x.
Always read the units from the axes to interpret what the gradient means.

Distance-time graphs:
Gradient = change in distance / change in time = speed.
A steeper line means greater speed. A horizontal line means the object is stationary. A negative gradient means the object is moving back towards the starting point.

Velocity-time graphs:
Gradient = change in velocity / change in time = acceleration.
Positive gradient = accelerating. Negative gradient = decelerating. Horizontal line = constant velocity. The area under a velocity-time graph = distance travelled.

For curves (non-linear graphs):
The gradient changes at different points. To find the gradient at a point, draw a tangent (a straight line that just touches the curve at that point) and calculate its gradient.

Estimating the gradient of a curve:
Step 1: Mark the point on the curve.
Step 2: Draw a tangent at that point (using a ruler).
Step 3: Choose two clear points on the tangent and calculate rise over run.

Other examples: on a unit-price graph, gradient = cost per unit. On a temperature-time graph, gradient = rate of temperature change.

worked_example.question: A distance-time graph shows a straight line from (0, 0) to (4, 100). Calculate the speed and describe the motion.
steps: Gradient = change in distance / change in time.
Change in distance = 100 - 0 = 100 m. Change in time = 4 - 0 = 4 s.
Speed = 25 m/s. The object is moving at a constant speed of 25 m/s.

common_mistakes: Reading the gradient as change in x over change in y instead of change in y over change in x.
Forgetting to include units — always state the units of the gradient based on the axis labels.
When drawing a tangent on a curve, making it cross the curve instead of just touching it — a tangent only touches the curve at exactly one point.
